questions.question: Why can ID generation become a system bottleneck?
options: Because some strategies centralize coordination or assume properties like ordering that are expensive to preserve.
Because IDs can only be created after a cache miss.
Because globally unique IDs require no clocks or metadata.
Because random numbers always arrive sorted.
questions.explanation: Ordering, coordination, and throughput all shape the cost of ID generation.

questions.question: What is a common reason to choose a Snowflake-style ID over a DB sequence?
options: It scales local generation while preserving rough time order without a single central allocator on every write.
It guarantees gapless numbering globally.
It removes any need to reason about clocks.
It is required for all public APIs.
questions.explanation: Snowflake-style IDs balance coordination cost and rough sortability.

questions.question: What is the main operational risk in a Snowflake-style generator?
options: Clock regressions or worker identity mistakes can create collisions or stalls.
It cannot support more than one region.
It forces every ID through a database transaction.
It always reveals less than random IDs.
questions.explanation: Snowflake-style designs depend on safe worker identity and time handling.

questions.question: Why might random IDs be a poor storage primary key even when they are good public IDs?
options: They can damage write locality and index behavior compared with more ordered keys.
They cannot be represented as strings.
They only work in one shard.
They prevent uniqueness checks.
questions.explanation: Randomness helps avoid guessability but may hurt storage locality.

questions.question: What trade-off comes with a central DB sequence?
options: It gives simple ordering semantics but can become a hot dependency at high write rates.
It removes all operational state from the system.
It is impossible to reason about in a single region.
It guarantees unguessable public IDs.
questions.explanation: A central allocator is easy to explain, but scale and availability can become concerns.

questions.question: Which metric is most useful for detecting a Snowflake generator problem before it becomes a collision incident?
options: Timestamp regression or generator-stall count by region and worker.
Browser tab count.
Markdown line length.
Only total database size.
questions.explanation: Time anomalies are a leading indicator for Snowflake-style generation trouble.

questions.question: When is decoupling storage key from public ID especially attractive?
options: When you want storage locality internally but do not want externally visible IDs to reveal sequence or volume.
When you want every system to share the same weak randomness source.
When you need IDs only during local testing.
When write volume is zero.
questions.explanation: Separate internal and external IDs can balance locality and security properties.

questions.question: What is the best first clarification when asked to design an ID generator?
options: Ask whether IDs need uniqueness only, or also ordering, locality, and low guessability.
Assume UUIDs solve every downstream need.
Start by choosing a bit layout before discussing requirements.
Avoid asking about throughput.
questions.explanation: Those requirements directly determine which ID strategy fits.
